Audit pass over `doc/source/development/internals.rst` — the page hadn't been touched substantively since 2023 and several claims are now wrong.

- `Index` was described as *""an ndarray of object dtype assuming nothing about its contents""*. Since 2.0 (GH-51111) `Index` holds any supported dtype natively (e.g. `pd.Index([1,2,3]).dtype == int64`).

- `DatetimeIndex` / `TimedeltaIndex` were described as having *""impl are the int64 values""* (with `in64` typo on the TDI line). They're backed by `DatetimeArray` / `TimedeltaArray`. `PeriodIndex` is on the same model and is now described in parallel.

- `date_range` and `period_range` were described as returning *""An ndarray of Python datetime objects""* / *""An ndarray of Period objects""*. They return `DatetimeIndex` and `PeriodIndex` respectively.
- Fix `cls._values` **refers is** the ""best possible"" array → `cls._values` **is** the ""best possible"" array.
Scope was kept tight to corrections only; no rewrite of the Values section or new BlockManager content.
- [ ] Sphinx build of `doc/source/development/internals.rst` renders without warnings.

- Annotates the comparison (`__eq__`/`__ne__`/`__lt__`/...), arithmetic (`__add__`/`__radd__`/...), and logical (`__and__`/`__or__`/...) dunders on `OpsMixin` with `-> Self` (and `-> tuple[Self, Self]` for `__divmod__`/`__rdivmod__`).

- Closes #40762: mypy and pyright now infer `Series` for `ser == ""a""` instead of `Any`. Same for `DataFrame`, and most `ExtensionArray` subclasses where `_cmp_method`/`_arith_method`/`_logical_method` returns `Self`.

- Three small fallout fixes for sites mypy now sees more precisely: `RangeIndex.__floordiv__` (returns parent `Index`, not `Self` — needs `# type: ignore[override]`); `pct_change` return path through `_LocIndexer` widens the type, needs `# type: ignore[return-value]`; `stata._stata_elapsed_date_to_datetime_vec` `.array.view(\""Period[Y]\"")` resolves to a union that doesn't have `.asfreq`, needs `# type: ignore[union-attr]`.

`Index` and `BaseMaskedArray` (where `_cmp_method` actually returns `ndarray` / `BooleanArray`, not `Self`) get a small typing approximation under this change — `idx == x` is reported as `Index` rather than `ndarray`. This doesn't break common usage (`.any()`, boolean indexing) and keeps the change minimal; explicit overrides on those classes can be a follow-up if needed.
- [x] `mypy` on the original repro from GH-40762 (`res: pd.Series = df['kind'] == 'a'`) — error is gone, runtime returns `Series`.
- [x] `pyright` on the same repro — `res` typed as `Series`.
- [x] Net mypy error count over pandas internal source: 442 → 442 (no regressions).

- Adds short docstrings to `OpsMixin.__and__/__or__/__xor__` (and the reverse variants) describing their elementwise logical/bitwise behavior. Inherited unchanged by `Series`, `DataFrame`, `Index`, and `ExtensionArray`.

- Adds a `.. note::` to the ""Combining / joining / set operations"" section of `doc/source/reference/indexing.rst` clarifying that these operators were aliases for set operations prior to pandas 2.0 (GH-37374) and now perform elementwise logical/bitwise ops, with a pointer to `Index.intersection` / `Index.union` / `Index.symmetric_difference` for the set-op behavior.
The original issue asked where the docs were for `&`, `
column2: `, `^` on `Index`. The semantics change in 2.0 fixed the underlying ambiguity but didn't surface anything user-discoverable on the Index reference page; this PR closes that documentation gap.

column1: - [x] Verified docstrings are visible on `pd.Index.__and__/__or__/__xor__` after rebuild (the `@functools.wraps` in `unpack_zerodim_and_defer` preserves them).

- [ ] Doc build picks up the new `.. note::` block in the rendered Index reference page.",,"{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65511/reactions', 'total_count': 0, '+1': 0, '-1': 0, 'laugh': 0, 'hooray': 0, 'confused': 0, 'heart': 0, 'rocket': 0, 'eyes': 0}",https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65511/timeline,,,,,,

- Setitem on nullable masked arrays (``Int64`` / ``Float64`` / ``boolean`` / ...) silently coerced list-like values that the equivalent scalar setitem already rejects. For example, ``arr[[0]] = ['1']`` parsed the string into the numeric dtype, and ``arr[[0]] = [True]`` on ``Int64`` / ``Float64`` coerced the boolean to ``1``. This was flagged by long-commented-out assertions in ``test_indexing.py``'s ``_check_setitem_invalid`` helper (FIXME comment from GH-45404, which fixed the scalar case but explicitly left the non-scalar case open).

- Adds ``BaseMaskedArray._validate_listlike``, which validates and coerces non-scalar setitem values. Compatible numeric/bool ndarrays still defer to ``_coerce_to_array`` (preserving the long-standing bool-as-int treatment that ``Series.mask`` / ``where`` rely on); other inputs are routed through ``_cast_pointwise_result`` to infer their natural type, and a dtype-kind mismatch raises ``TypeError``. No pure-Python iteration in any path.
- Uncomments the previously-disabled assertions in ``_check_setitem_invalid`` and adds a whatsnew entry.
- [x] ``pytest pandas/tests/extension/ pandas/tests/series/ pandas/tests/frame/ pandas/tests/indexing/`` (69356 passed, no regressions)

`Timestamp.to_julian_date` and `DatetimeIndex.to_julian_date` were computing the Julian date from the **local wall-clock** representation, so two timestamps referring to the same UTC instant in different timezones returned different values:

JD is an absolute-time concept (cf. `Timestamp.timestamp` / POSIX seconds, which already returns the UTC instant), so the fix routes tz-aware inputs through the naive UTC instant before computing. Behavior for tz-naive inputs is unchanged.

The implementation is a single early-return in each method — naturally vectorized for the array path via `tz_convert(""UTC"").tz_localize(None)`, no per-element Python loop. Supersedes the stale GH-60280.
- [x] New parametrized scalar tests covering UTC / US/Pacific / Europe/London
- [x] New parametrized array tests, including agreement with element-wise `Timestamp.to_julian_date`
- [x] Existing naive-only tests in `test_to_julian_date.py` (scalar + array) still pass

- [x] Full sweep of `pandas/tests/scalar/timestamp/`, `pandas/tests/indexes/datetimes/methods/`, and `pandas/tests/tools/test_to_datetime.py` — 4439 passed (the latter exercises `Timestamp(0).to_julian_date()` inside `to_datetime`)

`SeriesGroupBy._wrap_applied_output`'s Series/DataFrame branch tried to insert the grouper as a column when `as_index=False and not_indexed_same`, but after `_concat_objects` the result has more rows than groups (e.g. `n_groups * n_resample_bins` for `groupby().resample()`), so `DataFrame.insert` raised `ValueError: Length of values (5) does not match length of index (25)`.
`DataFrameGroupBy._wrap_applied_output_series` already returns straight from `_concat_objects` in this same situation (see GH-8467); mirror that here.

After the fix, `gb.agg({""b"": ""min""})` matches `gb.min()`'s shape on the same `groupby(as_index=False).resample(...)` object — both drop the group key from the index. As discussed on the issue (GH-52397), how `as_index=False` should interact with resampler is itself a separate question; this PR only stops the raise.

The xlrd and pyxlsb engines emit `Pandas4Warning` and are slated for removal (GH-56542; deprecated in 3.1.0). The user guide and install table still presented them as the standard way to read `.xls` and `.xlsb` files. This PR updates the docs to point users at `python-calamine` instead.
- Excel intro now recommends calamine for `.xls`/`.xlsb`.
- Engine auto-selection note describes the actual fallback logic (xlrd/pyxlsb if installed → calamine otherwise) and flags the trajectory.
- Removed the example that taught users to call `xlrd.open_workbook(..., on_demand=True)` and pass the resulting `xlrd.book.Book` to `ExcelFile`.

- Removed a stale claim in the writer note that `engine='xlrd'` produces an Excel 2003 workbook — xlrd is read-only and pandas has no `.xls` writer (only openpyxl, xlsxwriter, and odf are registered as writers).
- ""Binary Excel (.xlsb) files"" section now leads with calamine; pyxlsb demoted to a deprecation note.
- `getting_started/install.rst` — optional-deps Excel table annotates xlrd and pyxlsb as `(deprecated; use python-calamine)`.
- Drive-by: fix a typo in the `read_sas` iterator example — `chunk=` was not a valid kwarg, the correct one is `chunksize=`.
- [ ] Rendered pages read sensibly (Excel intro, engine note, .xlsb section, install table)

GH-49828 reports that `pd.NA and X`, `pd.NA or X`, and `not pd.NA` all raise `TypeError: boolean value of NA is ambiguous`, while the mirrored forms (`True and pd.NA`, `False or pd.NA`, etc.) sometimes succeed. This is not a bug — it's the unavoidable interaction of two design points:
1. `bool(pd.NA)` raises by design (GH-38224) — pandas refuses to guess truthiness of an unknown.
2. Python's `and`/`or`/`not` keywords always call `bool()` on their operand and cannot be overridden via dunders.
The asymmetry users see is just Python short-circuiting: `and`/`or` only call `bool()` on the **left** operand, so when `pd.NA` happens to be on the right, `bool()` is never invoked.

column2: `/`~` work correctly via `__and__`/`__or__`/`__invert__` and already implement Kleene logic.

column1: This PR adds a paragraph to the `NA in a boolean context` section of the user guide spelling out exactly this: why the keywords can't work, why the position-dependent behavior happens, and that `&`/`
I considered also expanding the `TypeError` message itself with the `&`/`
column2: `/`~` hint, but `bool(pd.NA)` is hit from many contexts (`if pd.NA:`, `assert pd.NA`, library code doing `if x == y:`, etc.) where that hint would be misleading. The docs are the right venue for the explanation.

A handful of small, independent docstring fixes surfaced by an audit of the public API. All changes are in docstrings only — no behavior changes.
- **`DataFrame.apply` / `Series.map`**: fix `numba.jit(nogit=True)` typo to `nogil=True`; add missing ""as"" in ""such Numba"".
- **`read_pickle`**: file-like object must implement `read()`, not `readlines()` — pickle calls `read()`.
- **`to_pickle`**: drop the Python 2.x protocol enumeration; just describe the current default.
- **`read_xml.dtype`**: drop reference to a non-existent `na_values` parameter (`read_xml` has none).
- **`read_html.skiprows`**: replace garbled phrase ""after parsing the column integer"" with ""after parsing the header"".
- **`Series.map`**: replace lingering `arg` references with `func` in the `**kwargs` description and Notes section.
- **`read_excel.dtype_backend`**: remove blank line that was breaking the rendering of the `pyarrow` bullet's continuation.
- **`DataFrame.set_index.verify_integrity`**: fill in the empty `.. deprecated:: 3.0.0` block with the same text used by the runtime `Pandas4Warning`.

- **`read_sql`**: move the `.. versionadded:: 2.2.0` directive out of the Examples section (where it was wedged between two doctests) into the `con` parameter description, where ADBC is first introduced.

- **`Series.groupby`**: fix DataFrame-isms in `by`, `as_index`, and `sort` parameter descriptions — these were copied verbatim from `DataFrame.groupby` and referenced ""selected axis"", ""Only relevant for DataFrame input"", and ""the original DataFrame"".
- [ ] No doctest changes needed (text-only edits to descriptions)

df3.max(axis=1) # Fails, as soon as we have a NaT

In dataframes with datetime64 columns, max() fails with a `TypeError: '>=' not supported between instances of 'Timestamp' and 'float'` when 2 different timezones and NaT values are both present. As long as we have 1 timezone or no NaTs, everything works as expected. The default behavior of max() is skipping nans, and NaT is interpreted as such correctly with the same timezone. Once the timezone is different, it is treated as a float and the comparison fails.

It seems like during the max() operation, since timezones differ, rows are coerced into an `object` dtype. The NaT is leaking into the underlying numpy amax as a float instead of being safely skipped or masked out before the comparison occurs.
Truly skipping NaTs as a default in max() before attempting the comparison of the remaining datetime64 values.

'merged_at': '2026-05-07T16:10:34Z'}","In https://github.com/apache/arrow/pull/49912 it was noticed that the trick of `sys.modules[""pyarrow""] = None` to let python think that pyarrow is not installed, does not work for imports in cython (while in case of a python `import pyarrow`, that gives a ModuleNotFoundError, which is an ImportError sublcass).

Now, this is not really a bug in pandas, but since we already have tried to import pyarrow in the `pandas.compat` module before we get to import `pandas._libs.lib`, we can also just reuse the knowledge of pyarrow being available or not, instead of doing a new try/except.
And that then also makes the `sys.modules` hack work again.

'merged_at': '2026-05-08T17:54:36Z'}","## Summary
Picks up the test-coverage work from the stale #54761 PR, scoped down to what is still needed against current main.

- ``algorithms._ensure_data`` actually unwraps complex ``NumpyExtensionArray`` to the underlying ndarray (current code is a no-op ``cast``), so hashtable-backed methods (``duplicated``, ``unique``, ``isin``, ...) dispatch correctly for complex-dtype EAs.

- ``NDArrayBackedExtensionArray.__setitem__`` translates numpy's complex-specific ``TypeError(\""only 0-dimensional arrays can be converted to Python scalars\"")`` into ``ValueError`` so error types match other numeric dtypes.

- ``complex128`` added to the parametrized arithmetic/ufunc tests in ``test_numeric.py``; ``test_ufunc_coercions`` rewritten so float and complex share one test body via per-parametrize ``exp_dtype``.
- ``\""complex\""`` added to the ``NumpyExtensionArray`` extension test fixture, with overrides where numpy semantics genuinely differ:
- cython backfill/pad isn't implemented for complex (xfail on ``test_fillna_no_op_returns_copy``).

- ``test_unstack`` xfailed for complex when the index isn't a uniform product — complex NaN fill correctly survives ``astype(object)`` as ``(nan+0j)``, which the base test's equivalence premise doesn't accommodate.

The previous PR also changed ``astype.py`` to coerce complex ``(nan+0j)`` to scalar ``np.nan`` on cast-to-object. That semantic change wasn't well-vetted in review and conflates \""valid complex with NaN component\"" with \""missing\"", so it's intentionally not included here; the unstack test is handled by xfail instead.
- [x] mypy clean on the three changed source files

'merged_at': '2026-05-06T22:18:59Z'}","## Summary
Removes two FIXME comments in `pandas/core/internals/managers.py` that have sat unaddressed since 2018 (GH-22028).

- `# FIXME: optimization potential` in `BaseBlockManager._combine` — I benchmarked the `np.sort(np.concatenate(...))` + `lib.get_reverse_indexer` pattern against `searchsorted` and `argsort`/inverse-rank alternatives. Wins were marginal (5–25% in wide-and-sparsely-filtered cases) and only on a path that isn't a meaningful hot spot. Not worth the added complexity.

- `# FIXME: refactor, clearly separate broadcasting & zip-like assignment` in `BlockManager.iset` — the comment wants the 1D-EA ""broadcast"" semantics split from the numpy/DTA-TDA ""zip-like"" semantics. Investigating, every caller (`isetitem`, `_set_item_mgr`, `_iset_item`, the internal `iset` recursion) passes a runtime-typed `ArrayLike` whose flavor is only known from `value.dtype`. So the dispatch genuinely has to live inside `iset` — splitting into top-level `_iset_broadcast`/`_iset_zip` would just turn `iset` into a 2-line dispatcher and relocate the same branch one level up. The current shape is structurally correct.

This PR adds `skipna` support to `SparseArray.mean()` for consistency with other pandas reduction operations.
TypeError: mean() got an unexpected keyword argument 'skipna'
because `SparseArray.mean()` did not explicitly support the `skipna` parameter.
* returns `np.nan` when missing values are present and `skipna=False`
* adds regression tests for both `skipna=True` and `skipna=False`
This PR is the second/revised version of my earlier PR, created with a clean branch and isolated history to keep the changes focused and easier to review.

Adds `skipna` support to `SparseArray.mean()` for consistency with other pandas reductions.
TypeError: mean() got an unexpected keyword argument 'skipna'
because the method did not explicitly support the parameter.
* returns `np.nan` when missing values are present and `skipna=False`
* adds regression tests for both `skipna=True` and `skipna=False`

I was unable to fully run the pandas test suite locally due to Windows build/environment issues, but the change is isolated and covered by targeted tests.","{'login': 'Manav-Vaddoriya', 'id': 181735435, 'node_id': 'U_kgDOCtUQCw', 'avatar_url': 'https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/181735435?v=4', 'gravatar_id': '', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya', 'html_url': 'https://github.com/Manav-Vaddoriya', 'followers_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/followers', 'following_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/following{/other_user}', 'gists_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/gists{/gist_id}', 'starred_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/starred{/owner}{/repo}', 'subscriptions_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/subscriptions', 'organizations_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/orgs', 'repos_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/repos', 'events_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/events{/privacy}', 'received_events_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/Manav-Vaddoriya/received_events', 'type': 'User', 'user_view_type': 'public', 'site_admin': False}","{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65484/reactions', 'total_count': 0, '+1': 0, '-1': 0, 'laugh': 0, 'hooray': 0, 'confused': 0, 'heart': 0, 'rocket': 0, 'eyes': 0}",https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65484/timeline,,,,,,

This change caches the per-column dtypes array on the manager and invalidates it alongside the existing _interleaved_dtype hook at every block-mutation site. get_dtypes returns a copy of the cache so df.dtypes (which wraps the array in a Series with copy=False) does not share storage across calls. When calling `get_indexer` with an ndarray containing `pd.NA` on an index containing `np.nan`, the results were inconsistent with the list input.

In `_maybe_cast_listlike_indexer`, when the target is a list, `ensure_index` normalizes `pd.NA` to `np.nan` via `Index.__new__` -> `sanitize_array`. However, when the target is already an ndarray (has `.dtype` attribute), this normalization was skipped.
Added handling in `_maybe_cast_listlike_indexer` to fillna `pd.NA` to `np.nan` when both self and target have object dtype, ensuring consistent matching behavior.
# Before fix: ndarray path returned -1, list path returned 0

'merged_at': '2026-05-07T16:18:47Z'}","## Summary
Audit of the nine ``# FIXME: don't leave commented-out`` notes scattered across the test suite. Two became live assertions; the rest were dead code that's been removed.
- ``test_block_internals.test_construction_with_conversions``: re-enable the ``dt3`` column on both sides of the comparison, with ``.astype(""M8[s]"")`` on the ``date_range`` so the units line up.

- ``test_indexing`` (masked): ``arr[[0]] = [invalid]`` / ``np.array([invalid], dtype=object)`` behavior is inconsistent across ``boolean``/``Int64``/``Float64`` (some scalars silently coerce, others raise ``ValueError`` instead of ``TypeError``).
- ``test_period_index``: stale alternative computations of ``expected``; current test passes without them.

- ``test_format.test_info_repr_max_cols``: ``# test verbose overrides`` stub — ``DataFrame.info(verbose=...)`` overriding ``display.max_info_columns`` is already covered by ``test_info.test_info_max_cols``.

- ``test_stata.test_read_dta2``: the format-113 reader genuinely returns wrong values (``datetime_c`` not converted, ``1959-01-01`` decodes to ``0002-01-01``); only expressible as an xfail that locks in the bug.
The remaining FIXME (``test_string_column_above_2GB`` in ``test_parquet.py``) is intentionally left for a separate PR.
- [x] ``pytest`` over the other four touched files (no regressions)
- Replaces the long-commented-out `test_string_column_above_2GB` (originally needing >5GB RAM) with a buffer-aliased version that exercises the same int32-offset boundary using ~150MB.

- Constructs a logically >2GB `pa.string()` ChunkedArray from a single 150MB data buffer referenced by 20 single-string chunks. `pyarrow.ChunkedArray.combine_chunks()` on this input raises with the exact `offset overflow while concatenating arrays` error from GH-55606, confirming the test exercises the real failure mode rather than a proxy.

- Drives `pd.StringDtype(storage=""pyarrow"").__from_arrow__(...)` end-to-end. Since `pc.cast` to `large_string` preserves chunked structure (and pyarrow shares the underlying data buffers across the cast), physical RSS stays bounded.

This only covers the pyarrow-storage branch of `__from_arrow__`. The python-storage branch goes through `arr.to_numpy(zero_copy_only=False)` which materializes Python `str` objects, so buffer aliasing doesn't help there — that branch isn't reasonably testable at >2GB and isn't covered here.
- [ ] Confirm it runs in <5s and stays well under 1GB RSS

The function `enable_data_resource_formatter` in `pandas/io/formats/printing.py` does not have a docstring describing its purpose or behavior.
This function performs non-trivial logic, including:
- Detecting whether the code is running in an IPython environment
- Dynamically registering a formatter for the `application/vnd.dataresource+json` mimetype
- Enabling or disabling that  formatter based on the 'enable' parameter
- What this formatter is used for
- When this function should be called
This makes the function difficult to understand and maintain for contributors.
The current implementation currently has no docstring, so developers must read and interpret the source code to understand its purpose and behavior.
Adding a docstring would improve the documentation by:
- Clearly explaining the purpose of the function
- Describing the meaning of the `enable` parameter

This makes the function easier to understand, reduces the likelihood of misuse, and improves maintainability for contributors. This PR adds boilerplate for SIMD detection, ~~feature toggle and a CI job disabling SIMD~~.
Was part of #64905 and added a few modifications:
- Created a simd directory with the goal to have all SIMD logic encapsulated there.
- It always defines `PANDAS_HAVE_SCALAR` so that in case our SIMD dispatch logic if flawed won't cause SIGILL.

- Uses `dependency` instead of `subproject` for xsimd, but requires a minimum version of `xsimd` of `>=14.0` to don't have to differentiate between `xsimd::common` and `xsimd::generic`; and requires the latest `xsimd` `>=14.2` for MSVC + ARM compatibility.
It doesn't add any SIMD code, the main visible change is the creation of `pandas_simd_config.h`

'merged_at': '2026-05-05T16:36:10Z'}","Closes #65462
Should be tested by our existing tests by removing the fsspec upper pin and updating the lock file
In the end this is actually an issue on our side and not a regression in fsspec:

`pandas.io.common._get_filepath_or_buffer` opened fsspec URLs with `fsspec.open(...).open()`, which thus immediately discards the intermediate `OpenFile` object (the result of the first `open()` call).

And fsspec documents that the `OpenFile` must stay alive while the returned file handle is in use. And because of an upstream fix, dropping it early now causes reads to fail with `ValueError: seek of closed file` with a Tar filesystem.
So the change here keeps that object alive, and returns it in the `IOArgs`, and then ensures we explicitly call `close()` on that object as well.

Add a defensive invariant check in the vendored ujson decoder's `decode_string()` function, guarding the `memcpy` that copies existing escape-buffer contents when promoting from stack to heap allocation.

`decode_string` promotes `ds->escStart` from the stack buffer to heap storage when the remaining input is larger than the current escape buffer. The existing code allocates `newSize * sizeof(wchar_t)` bytes and then copies `escLen * sizeof(wchar_t)` bytes from the previous buffer.

The surrounding control flow (line 715: `if ((size_t)(ds->end - ds->start) > escLen)`) already guarantees that `newSize > escLen`, so the copy destination is large enough. This check makes the invariant explicit and returns a distinct error message (""Internal string buffer size invariant violated"") if it is ever broken by a future refactor.
- Makes a subtle size relationship self-documenting
- Distinguishes invariant violations from allocation failures in error messages
- No behavioral change for valid or currently-reachable invalid inputs
- No reproducer exists for an actual overflow in the current code
- No test is added because the condition is believed unreachable under the current control flow
- The check is purely defensive hardening for future-proofing

'merged_at': '2026-05-11T08:43:07Z'}","Greetings!
This PR addresses the tracker at issue #63207
This is the deprecation warning (with test) and docstring update for the inplace keyword of Dataframe.rename.
If this looks alright, I can do the same for the other DataFrame methods mentioned in the tracker for which the inplace keyword is deprecated.

I generally followed the approach I saw for the deprecation of the verify_integrity keyword in the DataFrame.set_index method, I hope that's the preferred way but can change to something else if that's better.

(There is also a deprecate_kwarg decorator but the message it generates might be misleading for this case because it can be interpreted as ""this keyword is deprecated in general"" rather than ""this keyword is deprecated for this method"").

I added a test to check that the warning is triggered whenever the inplace keyword is used. For existing tests that check whether inplace works correctly, I added pytest.mark.filterwarnings so the new warning does not incorrectly fail them.
All pre-commit checks passed or were skipped.
Something I'd like to mention to be sure that this is correct behavior:
I ran pytest pandas locally on this feature branch and my local main branch (which I checked has no diff to upstream/main).
For both of them, I have the same 7 failures and all other results (save the additional test for this warning) are the same. In particular:
I assumed this meant the changes did not cause the 7 failures but want to make sure.

'merged_at': '2026-05-04T15:07:13Z'}","Bumps [github/codeql-action](https://github.com/github/codeql-action) from 4.35.2 to 4.35.3.

• Upcoming breaking change: Add a deprecation warning for customers using CodeQL version 2.19.3 and earlier. These versions of CodeQL were discontinued on 9 April 2026 alongside GitHub Enterprise Server 3.15, and will be unsupported by the next minor release of the CodeQL Action. #3837
• Configurations for private registries that use Cloudsmith or GCP OIDC are now accepted. #3850

• Best-effort connection tests for private registries now use GET requests instead of HEAD for better compatibility with various registry implementations. For NuGet feeds, the test is now always performed against the service index. #3853
• Fixed a bug where two diagnostics produced within the same millisecond could overwrite each other on disk, causing one of them to be lost. #3852
See the releases page for the relevant changes to the CodeQL CLI and language packs.

• Upcoming breaking change: Add a deprecation warning for customers using CodeQL version 2.19.3 and earlier. These versions of CodeQL were discontinued on 9 April 2026 alongside GitHub Enterprise Server 3.15, and will be unsupported by the next minor release of the CodeQL Action. #3837
• Configurations for private registries that use Cloudsmith or GCP OIDC are now accepted. #3850

• Best-effort connection tests for private registries now use GET requests instead of HEAD for better compatibility with various registry implementations. For NuGet feeds, the test is now always performed against the service index. #3853
• Fixed a bug where two diagnostics produced within the same millisecond could overwrite each other on disk, causing one of them to be lost. #3852

• The undocumented TRAP cache cleanup feature that could be enabled using the CODEQL_ACTION_CLEANUP_TRAP_CACHES environment variable is deprecated and will be removed in May 2026. If you are affected by this, we recommend disabling TRAP caching by passing the trap-caching: false input to the init Action. #3795
• The Git version 2.36.0 requirement for improved incremental analysis now only applies to repositories that contain submodules. #3789

• Python analysis on GHES no longer extracts the standard library, relying instead on models of the standard library. This should result in significantly faster extraction and analysis times, while the effect on alerts should be minimal. #3794
• Fixed a bug in the validation of OIDC configurations for private registries that was added in CodeQL Action 4.33.0 / 3.33.0. #3807
• Fix incorrect minimum required Git version for improved incremental analysis: it should have been 2.36.0, not 2.11.0. #3781
• Reduced the minimum Git version required for improved incremental analysis from 2.38.0 to 2.11.0. #3767
• Downgrade default CodeQL bundle version to 2.24.3 due to issues with a small percentage of Actions and JavaScript analyses. #3762

• Added an experimental change which disables TRAP caching when improved incremental analysis is enabled, since improved incremental analysis supersedes TRAP caching. This will improve performance and reduce Actions cache usage. We expect to roll this change out to everyone in March. #3569
• We are rolling out improved incremental analysis to C/C++ analyses that use build mode none. We expect this rollout to be complete by the end of April 2026. #3584

Upcoming change: Starting April 2026, the CodeQL Action will skip collecting file coverage information on pull requests to improve analysis performance. File coverage information will still be computed on non-PR analyses. Pull request analyses will log a warning about this upcoming change. #3562

• Repositories owned by an organization: Create a custom repository property with the name github-codeql-file-coverage-on-prs and the type "True/false", then set this property to true in the repository's settings. For more information, see Managing custom properties for repositories in your organization. Alternatively, if you are using an advanced setup workflow, you can set the CODEQL_ACTION_FILE_COVERAGE_ON_PRS environment variable to true in your workflow.
• User-owned repositories using default setup: Switch to an advanced setup workflow and set the CODEQL_ACTION_FILE_COVERAGE_ON_PRS environment variable to true in your workflow.
• 262a15f Add generic non-printable chars test for OIDC configs

When a `numpy.ma.MaskedArray` column is stored in a DataFrame and groups are iterated via `groupby(..., observed=False)`, the type returned by `Series.values` depends on whether the rows happen to be pre-sorted by the groupby key and on whether the DataFrame is empty. The same column, same dtype, same group, just different row ordering or DataFrame size, produces either `ndarray` or `MaskedArray`.

`Series.values` should return a consistent type regardless of whether the underlying DataFrame rows are sorted, unsorted, or empty. Given the column dtype is numeric (e.g. `float64`), `ndarray` is the expected return type in all three cases.

'merged_at': None}","This PR fixes an inconsistency reported in #22921 where merging an empty DataFrame with a non-empty DataFrame via `left_on`/`right_index` (or `left_index`/`right_on`) inadvertently preserved the named index from the non-empty DataFrame.

Previously, when one side was empty, `_get_join_info` bypassed standard join index creation and fell back to `copy()`/`take()` on the populated axis, dragging along its index metadata. This updates the logic to construct a fresh `default_index(n)` when one side is empty. The length `n` is correctly calculated based on the `how` parameter (e.g., `n = len(right)` for an outer join with an empty left DataFrame).
Parametrized tests have been added to ensure consistency across all join types (`inner`, `left`, `right`, `outer`).

different things depending on the accessor, the key shape, the dtype, and the block structure of unrelated columns:
# np.array RHS — yet another set of outcomes
Six open issues describe pieces of this directly. Filing as a single umbrella so they can be considered together.

**#16864 (2017) — One item lists are assigned into dataframes as single items.** `df.loc[[0,1], 'new_col'] = [['one_item'], ['one_item']]` stores `'one_item'` (the inner string) in each row instead of `['one_item']` (the list). The reporter wanted to populate a list-valued column row-by-row and was surprised that single-element nested lists got unwrapped while multi-element ones didn't.

**#25806 (2019) — Effect of assigning list to DataFrame cell depends on unrelated column's type.** `df.loc['a', 'foo'] = ['123']` stores `['123']` on a fresh DataFrame, but the *same* assignment on the same DataFrame stores `'123'` once an unrelated column has been cast to float. Behavior depends on the block layout, not on user intent. Same example shows `.at` and `.loc` diverging on the same input.

**#30649 (2019) — `df.at[...] = ['some list']` doesn't set object dtype on column creation.** Setting a list via `.at` to a non-existent column creates the column with the wrong dtype; the first row stores the unwrapped scalar `'a'` while subsequent rows (after the column exists) correctly store `['a']`. Same accessor, same RHS, two different outcomes depending on whether the column already exists.

**#35006 (2020) — Different behavior when setting a list(scalar) to entire column vs filtered rows.** `df[""new""] = list_of_arrays` produces one outcome; `df.loc[mask, ""new""] = list_of_arrays` produces another, even when the mask matches every row. Multi-step repro showing four ""expected"" cases and two ""unexpected"" ones with the same RHS construction.

**#53565 (2023, regression from 2.0) — Reassigning a Series index with a 1-D length-1 ndarray loses dimension.** First `s['whatever'] = np.array([1])` stores shape `(1,)` as expected. *Second* assignment of the same value collapses to shape `()`. Reproducible 1.5 → 2.0 regression, no movement since.

**#57923 (2024) — Inconsistent implicit conversion of 1-arrays inside DataFrames.** `df.at[0, 0] = np.arange(2)` stores `array([0, 1])`; `df.at[1, 0] = np.arange(1)` stores `np.int64(0)`. Length-1 ndarrays get silently unwrapped to scalars where length-2+ ndarrays don't, on the same accessor with the same dtype. Reporter notes pandas can be tricked into holding 1-arrays via `applymap`, demonstrating the unwrap is at the setitem boundary and not a storage limitation.
- **#26333 (2019)** — REGR: assigning a tuple/scalar-with-length to an object-dtype cell stopped working in 0.23, ""Must have equal len keys and value when setting with an iterable"".
- **#46544 (2022)** — `DataFrame.loc` and `DataFrame.__setitem__` give different results for the same 2-D ndarray RHS.
- **#57962 (2024)** — `df.loc[1, 2] = np.zeros(3)` into an object-dtype cell raises despite the dtype permitting it (1.4 → 2.x regression).
- **#61223 (2025)** — `.at` setting an iterable fails when the column doesn't yet exist, succeeds afterwards.
- **#15490 (2017)** — `.at` / `.loc` modifies the DataFrame even when the assignment raises.
- **#45769 (2022)** — MultiIndex `loc` with an n×2 list assignment fails despite shape match.
- **#62369 (2025)** — API question on what dtype setitem-with-expansion should infer.
- **#55935 (2023)** — PDEP-6: setitem casting (the standing umbrella for setitem semantic decisions).
- **#27911 (2019)** — API: when to cast list-like to ndarray, check len match (the arithmetic-side analog of this question).
- **#60218 (2024)** — ERR: consistent error messages for unsupported setitem values.
- **#42549 (2021)** — list-like objects broadcast to each row (1.3 regression caused by `is_list_like` not detecting some sequences).
- **#46974 (2022)** — DataFrame setitem with DataFrame RHS doesn't align column names.
- **#51386 (2023)** — `Series.__setitem__` with Series value: alignment vs replacement question.
- **#53996 (2023)** — DOC: is the behavior of setitem with repeated keys defined?
- **#57765 (2024)** — Setting a 2-D numpy array as a column uses only the first column.
- **#61026 (2025)** — Setting column with 2-D object-dtype array raises.

Original-intent context for the carve-outs that any fix would need to revisit: **#19474** (the explicit unwrap-on-listlike-LHS), **#37629** (nested-container preservation), **#44103** (force-split-path for scalar-row + listlike-col).

These six bull's-eye issues are not separate bugs in separate corners — they're the same inconsistency surfacing at six different accessor / dtype / shape / expansion-vs-existing combinations, on a code path that has accumulated independent decision points at multiple layers. Discussing them in one place makes the matrix visible and lets any fix (or deprecation) be evaluated against all six at once rather than one at a time.",,"{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65452/reactions', 'total_count': 0, '+1': 0, '-1': 0, 'laugh': 0, 'hooray': 0, 'confused': 0, 'heart': 0, 'rocket': 0, 'eyes': 0}",https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65452/timeline,,,"{'total': 0, 'completed': 0, 'percent_completed': 0}","{'blocked_by': 0, 'total_blocked_by': 0, 'blocking': 0, 'total_blocking': 0}",,

A handful of test FIXMEs other than the ""don't leave commented-out"" batch were addressable. The remainder are still load-bearing (real bugs / open issues / architectural inconsistencies) and were left in place.

- **`groupby/aggregate/test_other.py`** — restored `gb.agg(sum)` coverage. The FIXME's claim that this raises when `numeric_only=False` is passed no longer applies (verified: `gb.agg(sum)` returns the expected result).
- **`groupby/aggregate/test_numba.py`** — removed bare `# FIXME` next to a `pytest.param`; the adjacent `xfail(reason=...)` already documents the missing feature.
- **`indexes/multi/test_reshape.py`** — both sides of the comparison are now `int64` (no float coercion from intermediate NaN insertion); dropped the FIXME and `check_dtype=False`.

- **`io/parser/test_encoding.py`** — pyarrow no longer *freezes* on utf-16/32 with `pass_encoding=True`; verified it now raises a `ParserError` (""straddling object straddles two block boundaries""). Replaced the ""this is bad!"" FIXME with a concrete description. Kept as `skip` rather than `xfail` because the failure path takes ~9s per case (~110s × 12 cases).
- `io/test_orc.py:322` — ORC roundtrip still loses non-ns resolution; the cast is a real workaround.

- `frame/test_constructors.py:2655/2666` — verified empirically that with CoW, `df.iloc[:, 2] = ...` for an EA column still mutates the original `c` when `copy=False`. GH-35417 was about `df[col] = arr`, not iloc.
- `arrays/period/test_constructors.py:124` — `_from_sequence(asi8.astype(object))` still treats ints as years rather than ordinals (GH-64227 still open).
- `indexes/test_any_index.py:54` — stylistic note; the object-dtype string branch is still needed because `.map` infers to `str`.

Cleans up FIXME comments inherited from upstream ujson. Three are dropped as obsolete, one is repurposed into an actual user-facing improvement, and the remaining four are annotated as upstream-origin so they don't read as pandas-specific TODOs.
- `JSONtoObj.c`: decode errors now include a byte offset, e.g. `Expected object or value at position 5`. The `errorOffset` field was already populated by `SetError`; we just weren't surfacing it.

- `ultrajsondec.c`: drop the two `BREAK_FRC_LOOP` / `BREAK_EXP_LOOP` ""Check for arithmetic overflow"" FIXMEs. `intValue` overflow is already guarded in the integer loop above; `expValue` is a `double`, so `pow(10, huge)` yields `inf` rather than UB.
- `ultrajsonenc.c`: drop the obsolete ""Perhaps implement recursion detection"" FIXME — `encode()` already enforces `enc->recursionMax`.

- Remaining four FIXMEs (`escBuffer` pre-sizing, `g_asciiOutputTable` magic values, `Buffer_Realloc` cross-call sizing, ""Handle integration functions returning NULL"") are still present verbatim in upstream ultrajson `main` and are now annotated as such.
- [x] New parametrized test pinning the `at position N` suffix for three known bad inputs

'merged_at': '2026-05-03T17:30:39Z'}","Currently, ASV builds run in isolated environments, which causes the NumPy include directory path to change between builds. This prevents `ccache` from reusing cached compilation steps, making the benchmark build process unnecessarily slow.

Two changes targeting the compressed-file hot path in ``read_sas``:

1. **RLE/RDC decompression**: replace per-byte loops in ``rle_decompress`` and ``rdc_decompress`` with ``memcpy``/``memset`` for byte runs. Bounds are asserted once per opcode block instead of per byte. The RDC long-pattern path keeps the byte-by-byte loop for the overlap case (``ofs < cnt``) where intentional pattern repetition requires byte-by-byte semantics; the non-overlap case is ``memcpy``. ``process_byte_array_with_data`` drops the per-byte ``buf_get`` and uses ``memcpy`` for the column copy — this generalizes the ``lngt == 8`` fast path from #65363 with no extra branch.

2. **Per-page subheader pointers**: replace the Python list-of-tuples ``_current_page_data_subheader_pointers`` with two preallocated int64 arrays. Move the per-page subheader-walk loop out of Python's ``_process_page_metadata`` into a new module-level Cython ``collect_page_subheaders`` that reads pointer fields directly from the cached page (with inline byteswap), looks up the signature index via the existing 32/64-bit lookup tables, writes data subheaders into the int64 arrays, and only bounces non-data subheaders back to the Python processors. ``readline()`` then indexes the arrays directly with no GIL acquire and no tuple unpack. This is the win on compressed files where every page is a meta page and every row goes through a tuple lookup.

End-to-end vs. pyreadstat: ``test_meta2_page`` (the realistic many-rows-per-meta-page shape for compressed files) goes from 1.46× slower than readstat to **0.72×** (28% faster). The 10-row test files remain dominated by metadata parsing + DataFrame construction, which is the target of #65364.
- [x] All 117 SAS tests pass (covers RLE/RDC, mix/meta/data pages, compressed datetime, encoding, iterator paths)
- [x] Numeric output matches pyreadstat byte-for-byte across all compressed test fixtures

After GH-64798, `Index.str.split`, `Index.str.rsplit`, and `Index.str.findall` with `expand=False` raise `TypeError` on object/string-dtype Indexes, because the per-row Python lists are unhashable and can no longer be wrapped in an `Index`.

This restores functionality by returning an `ndarray[object]` of lists instead of attempting to construct an Index. Per Joris's suggestion in GH-64798 (returning an array, mirroring the historical precedent for some Index methods).

The `ArrowDtype(pa.string())` path is intentionally left untouched in this PR. That path has the same conceptual brokenness (the resulting Index of arrow lists fails on `get_loc` / `unique` / set ops), but it slipped through GH-64798 because `check_all_hashable` only fires on object dtype. We can address that separately if desired.

This PR fixes an inconsistency in `Index.get_indexer` where `pd.NA` was not matched against `NaN` labels in object-dtype indexes when the target was an `ndarray` or an `Index`.

The fix involves normalizing `pd.NA` to `np.nan` in `_maybe_cast_listlike_indexer` for object-dtype indexes, which aligns the behavior of array-like inputs with the existing behavior for list-like inputs and `get_loc` scalar lookups.
- Updated `pandas/core/indexes/base.py` to ensure consistent null normalization during indexer casting.
- Added a news entry in the `v3.1.0.rst` whatsnew file.

- Adds a regression test for GH-56996 covering `to_timedelta(np.int32(0), unit=""D"")` and similar sub-int64 NumPy integer scalars.

- The original `OutOfBoundsTimedelta` was incidentally fixed by GH-63302 (`Timedelta.__new__` integer branch now short-circuits through `np.timedelta64(value, unit)` for non-`ns` units, avoiding the NEP 50 path in `cast_from_unit`).
- No code change needed beyond the test — closes #56996.

Under NumPy 2 / NEP 50, `np.int32(x) - py_int` returns `np.int32` rather than a Python int. `cast_from_unit` then computed `frac * m` where `m` could exceed int32 range (e.g. `86_400_000_000_000` ns/day), raising `OutOfBoundsTimedelta`. The integer fast-path added in GH-63302 means that path is no longer reachable from `to_timedelta`/`Timedelta` for integer scalars with a non-`ns` unit, but a regression test ensures it stays that way.
- [x] `pytest pandas/tests/tools/test_to_timedelta.py` — 101 passed (including 4 new parametrized cases for `int16`, `int32`, `uint32`, `int64`).

- The originally-reported failure (`pd.concat([q_df, y_df], keys=[...], axis=1)` raising `IncompatibleFrequency` when the column `PeriodIndex` objects have different `freq`) no longer reproduces on `main`.

- It was fixed as a side effect of GH-61875, which made `IncompatibleFrequency` subclass `TypeError`. The `Categorical.__init__` factorize path that concat uses already had `try: factorize(values, sort=True) except TypeError: factorize(values, sort=False)`, so the freq mismatch is now caught and falls back to an unsorted object-dtype level, which is the desired behavior.
- Adds a regression test in `TestPeriodConcat` covering the reproducer from the issue.

- `Period.strftime` with an unknown directive (e.g. `""%Y-Q%Q""`, where the user meant `%q`) was passed straight to C `strftime`. On Windows, MSVCRT invokes its invalid-parameter handler and aborts the Python process. On glibc/macOS the output is silently garbled.

- This adds a pre-validation step in `_period_strftime` that rejects unknown `%X` directives with `ValueError(""Invalid format string"")` on all platforms. Pandas-specific directives (`%q`, `%f`, `%F`, `%l`, `%u`, `%n`) are pre-extracted before validation, so they are intentionally absent from the allowlist.
- The allowlist mirrors CPython's `time.strftime` Windows allowlist — a conservative cross-platform set of valid C `strftime` directives.
- [x] New parametrized regression test `test_strftime_invalid_format` covers `%Y-Q%Q`, `%Q`, `%E`, and trailing `%`.
- [x] Manually verified valid pandas-specific directives (`%q`, `%n`, etc.) and `Series.dt.strftime` still work; invalid directives now raise `ValueError`.

`DataFrame.__repr__` raises `TypeError` when a column holds a NumPy *structured* `ndarray` (as produced by `DataFrame.from_records` on a structured array, including nested field-of-fields dtypes). The previous fix for the related GH-48526 only routed `np.rec.recarray` to the structured-aware `_isna_recarray_dtype`; plain structured `ndarray` fell through to `np.isnan`, which does not support structured dtypes.

This PR broadens the dispatch in `_isna_array` from `isinstance(values, np.rec.recarray)` to `dtype.names is not None`, which matches both `np.rec.recarray` and structured `np.ndarray`. The body of `_isna_recarray_dtype` already handles both since it goes through `record.tolist()`.
- [x] `pandas/tests/frame/test_repr.py` — 65 passed (includes the four existing GH-48526 recarray repr tests)

- The iso8601 fast path required exactly 4 digits for the year, so strings like `""-111-01-01""` fell through to `dateutil`, which silently strips the leading `""-""` and parses as a positive year (so `Timestamp(""-111-01-01"") == Timestamp(""111-01-01"")` was `True`).
- Extend the iso8601 parser to accept 1-4 digit BC years, matching `numpy.datetime64` behavior.
- Raise `DateParseError` for non-iso strings starting with `""-""` (e.g. `""-12:30""`, `""--12-01-01""`) rather than letting `dateutil` silently mishandle them.
`to_datetime(""-0111-01-01"")` still mishandles the negative sign because it goes through `_guess_datetime_format` + `array_strptime`, a different code path. That's a separate pre-existing bug.
- [x] New `test_negative_year_iso8601` parametrized over 1-4 digit BC years

'merged_at': '2026-05-17T13:24:27Z'}","## Summary

- Deprecates the ``%n`` directive in ``Period.strftime``. ``%n`` clashes with the POSIX standard meaning of a newline character, which is confusing when ``Period.strftime`` is mixed with C strftime expectations.
- Adds ``%N`` as the replacement directive for nanoseconds. ``%n`` continues to work but emits a ``Pandas4Warning``.
- The default ns-frequency repr (no ``fmt`` passed, e.g. ``str(per)``) does not warn — only user-provided format strings containing ``%n`` trigger the warning.
- The vectorized array path (``PeriodArray._format_native_types`` / ``PeriodIndex.strftime`` / ``Series.dt.strftime``) emits the warning once per call rather than per element.
- [x] New ``Period.strftime`` tests for ``%N`` (no warning, correct nanoseconds), ``%n`` (Pandas4Warning, still produces nanoseconds), and the default no-fmt path (no warning).
- [x] New ``Series.dt.strftime`` tests on a ns-frequency PeriodIndex covering both ``%N`` and the deprecated ``%n``, verifying the warning fires once across the array.

Follow-up to GH-65094, addressing the [next-to-last review comment](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65094#issuecomment-3502023527) from @jorisvandenbossche.

GH-65094 removed the per-column casting loop in `_append_internal` under the assumption that `_post_expansion_casting` in `_LocationIndexer.__setitem__` made it redundant. That's not equivalent for two reasons:
1. **Correctness**: `_post_expansion_casting` rebuilds via `pd_array([], dtype=...)`, so EAs whose state lives on the *array* (e.g. geopandas CRS) lose that state.

2. **Performance**: when `find_common_type` falls back to `object` (e.g. `ArrowDtype(pa.struct(...))` + `object` row), concat materializes the whole column to object and post-cast rebuilds — Joris's 1M-row example regresses ~170× on this hardware.
This PR reintroduces the pre-cast loop, but conditionally:
- skip columns where the row's inferred dtype already matches the original (avoids the unconditional `_cast_pointwise_result` overhead the original loop paid)
- skip plain-object original columns (post-cast is a no-op anyway)

For columns we do pre-cast, the original `ExtensionArray`'s *instance* `_cast_pointwise_result` runs — which is what carries array-level state (CRS, etc.) into the row's value. Concat then takes the same-dtype fastpath and `_post_expansion_casting` is a no-op.
GH-65094's own 500-iter microbenchmark, on this machine:

The `int64`/`object` benchmarks keep GH-65094's gains because their dtype-equality / object-orig short-circuits fire first. The EA microbenchmarks regress 2–8% on the small-N hot path — the cost of restoring per-row pre-casting for the EA cases. I'd argue that's worth it to avoid the catastrophic regression on the large-N case GH-65094 was supposed to be improving in the first place.

- [x] Manually verified array-level state preservation with a synthetic geopandas-style EA (state on `array.tag`, analogous to CRS): `tag=""EPSG:4326""` survives the append on this PR, becomes `None` on current `main`.

I have not added a regression test in the repo — the geopandas case can't be tested directly without a new test dependency, and the closest synthetic reproduction needs a ~70-line custom `ExtensionArray`. Happy to add one if reviewers want — let me know which form you'd prefer.

'merged_at': '2026-05-01T11:26:45Z'}","## Summary
- Fixes the Code Checks job that has been failing on every PR since 2026-04-29 ~21:00 UTC with:
error    libmamba Could not solve for environment specs
The following package could not be installed
└─ types-pymysql =* * does not exist (perhaps a typo or a missing channel).

[mamba 2.6.0](https://github.com/mamba-org/mamba/releases/tag/2.6.0) was released on 2026-04-29 14:04 UTC and made **sharded repodata the default**. Shard keys on conda-forge are normalized to lowercase (the feedstock recipe sets ``package.name: {{ name
column2: lower }}``), and the new shard lookup is case-sensitive. Mixed-case entries in ``environment.yml`` therefore stop resolving — even though the packages still exist on the channel.

column1: The Unit Tests workflow is unaffected because it switched to pixi; only Code Checks (and the doc build) still go through ``setup-micromamba`` + ``environment.yml``.

The mamba-org/setup-micromamba action is already pinned by SHA in our workflow, but it downloads the latest micromamba binary on each run, which is why a pure upstream change broke us without any workflow edit.

'merged_at': '2026-04-30T16:58:15Z'}","## Summary
Final PR in the ref-array-no-freq series. Frequency is now Index-only state for ``DatetimeIndex`` / ``TimedeltaIndex``; ``DatetimeArray`` and ``TimedeltaArray`` no longer carry it.

- ``arr.freq`` / ``arr._freq`` / ``arr.freqstr`` now raise ``AttributeError`` on ``DatetimeArray`` / ``TimedeltaArray``. ``PeriodArray.freq`` is unchanged (derived from dtype). ``Series.dt.freq`` is unchanged (defined directly on the accessor).
- ``_pin_freq`` no longer reads a transitional stash from the array; it takes ``inferred`` as an argument, and the ``DatetimeIndex`` / ``TimedeltaIndex`` constructors compute it directly.
- Pickle migration in ``_new_DatetimeIndex`` / ``_new_TimedeltaIndex`` switched to ``vars(dta).pop(\""_freq\"", None)`` so legacy pickles that stored freq as an instance attr still migrate correctly.

- ``\""freq\""`` removed from ``DatetimeArray._datetimelike_ops`` / ``TimedeltaArray._datetimelike_ops`` (the ``delegate_names`` decorator would otherwise look it up on the array). ``DatetimeProperties`` and ``TimedeltaProperties`` already define their own ``freq`` property.

- ``check_freq`` removed from the internal ``assert_datetime_array_equal`` / ``assert_timedelta_array_equal`` asserters. ``check_freq`` on the public ``assert_series_equal`` / ``assert_frame_equal`` is **kept**, since the wrapped Index still carries freq.
- Test cleanups: removes ``arr._freq = None`` no-op writes and ``assert arr.freq is None`` assertions that are no longer meaningful.

'merged_at': '2026-05-07T16:21:33Z'}","- closes #55932

GH-55932 reports that the ``fold`` argument was ignored when ``tz`` was passed as a plain string (e.g. ``tz=\""US/Pacific\""``), while ``dateutil.tz.gettz(...)`` and ``zoneinfo.ZoneInfo(...)`` worked. The string was resolving to a ``pytz`` zone, and ``pytz.localize`` ignores ``fold``, so an ``AmbiguousTimeError`` was raised.

This was fixed as a side effect of GH-59089 (making pytz an optional dependency): ``maybe_get_tz`` now resolves a plain string to ``zoneinfo.ZoneInfo``, which honors PEP 495 ``fold``. This PR just adds a regression test to lock that behavior in — covering both reports in the issue thread (the ``year=/month=/...`` keyword form and the naive-datetime form).

'merged_at': None}","## Summary

- Fixes a label-equality check in `_get_merge_keys` that compared `lk == rk` directly. NaN compares unequal to itself, so `merge(left_on=np.nan, right_on=np.nan)` failed to drop the right-side duplicate column. `pd.NA` on either side raised `TypeError: boolean value of NA is ambiguous`.
- Uses `is_matching_na` plus a `TypeError` guard, mirroring the pattern already used in `pandas/core/ops/common.py`.
- Resolves the long-standing `# FIXME: what about other NAs?` comment introduced in GH-48590.
A related downstream inconsistency in `Index.get_indexer` / `Index.drop` for `pd.NA` vs the auto-coerced NaN column label is filed separately as GH-65419.
A second analogue of the same `lk == rk` pattern at `_maybe_restore_index_levels` (line 1252) is unaddressed here.
- [x] New parametrized `test_merge_on_mismatched_na_labels` (NaN vs `pd.NA`, NaN vs `NaT` — no drop, no raise)

- **Stale FIXME (line 1040)** — `_expand_ellipsis` already raises `IndexingError` if more than one Ellipsis is present (a few lines above), so the comment was no longer accurate. Removed.
- **Real bug (line 2471, GH-42099)** — `_setitem_new_column` seeded a brand-new column from a list-like value via `infer_fill_value(value)`, which produced wrong fill values for several common cases:

- `df.loc[[0, 1, 2], ""data""] = [""a"", ""b"", ""c""]` filled unselected rows with the literal string `""n""`. Chain: `np.asarray([\""a\"",\""b\"",\""c\""])` infers dtype `<U1`, then `np.array(np.nan, dtype=\""<U1\"")` casts `nan` to `\""nan\""` and truncates to width 1 → `\""n\""`. Wider strings would have produced `\""na\""`, `\""nan\""`, etc. — same root cause, less obviously wrong.
- `df.loc[[0, 1, 2], ""data""] = [True, False, True]` raised `TypeError` because the column was first initialized as float64, and writing booleans into it triggered `LossySetitemError`.

The fix mirrors the existing `is_array_like(value)` branch: convert the list to an array via `sanitize_array`, build an NA-filled column with `take_nd`, then write the values directly. The follow-up `_setitem_with_indexer` call is no longer needed for this branch since values are already written.

- After the all-NA-entry deprecation was enforced in GH-58314 (April 2024), `JoinUnit.is_na` only returns `True` for V-dtype placeholder blocks. That makes the body of `_is_valid_na_for` (object scan, NaT-cross-dtype, the `pd.NA`-vs-i8 FIXME kludge) unreachable: the very first check after `is_na` (`blk.dtype.kind == ""V""`) always fires, and the rest is dead code.
- The `upcasted_na is None` early-return and the object-block fill-value override in `get_reindexed_values` are likewise dead under the new `is_na` semantics.
- Drops `_is_valid_na_for`, simplifies `is_na` and `get_reindexed_values`, and prunes the now-unused imports (`NaT`, `NA`, `cast`, `needs_i8_conversion`, `is_valid_na_for_dtype`).

`Series.map()` on a categorical column with a `defaultdict` and `na_action=None`
incorrectly replaces `NA` values with `float(""NaN"")` instead of mapping them through
The non-categorical column `a` correctly returns `True`, but the categorical
column `b` returns `NaN` at row 2 instead of `True`.
In `Categorical.map` (`categorical.py`), the NA value was computed using
`mapper.get(np.nan, np.nan)` for dict-like mappers. For a `defaultdict`,
`.get()` bypasses `__missing__` and returns `None` instead of invoking the
default factory — that `None` then gets cast to `float(""NaN"")` during
for dict-like mappers, matching the behavior already used for callable mappers.
- `test_map_defaultdict_na_action_none` — verifies NA is passed through the default factory

'merged_at': '2026-04-28T23:56:43Z'}","## Summary

The `Pyodide build` CI job has been failing on `main` since pip 26.1 was released. `pyodide venv` generates a pip wrapper that runs on the host CPython but patches `sys.platform` to `""emscripten""` before invoking pip. pip 26.1 bumped its vendored urllib3 to a version whose `urllib3.contrib.emscripten` package imports `js` eagerly, so every pip invocation in the venv crashes with:

This is upstream issue [pyodide/pyodide-build#339](https://github.com/pyodide/pyodide-build/issues/339), fixed in [pyodide/pyodide-build#341](https://github.com/pyodide/pyodide-build/pull/341) by importing `pip._vendor.urllib3` before patching `sys.platform`. The fix is merged on `main` but not yet released.

Speeds up `pandas.tseries.frequencies.to_offset` string parsing. Stacked on top of GH-65390 (remove `_offset_map` cache); review/merge that first.

The big win is on tick-resolution offsets (""h"", ""min"", ""s"", ""ms"", ""us"", ""ns"", ""D""). The previous implementation built a `Timedelta(1, unit=name)`, called `delta_to_tick` to wrap it as a Tick, then multiplied by `float(stride)` — which calls `Tick.__mul__(float)`, which calls `np.isclose` on every invocation. That last step alone dominated profiles. Now we look the prefix up in a small `{name: (TickKlass, factor)}` dict and construct the Tick subclass directly for integer strides; fractional strides like ""2.5min"" still fall through to the old path so unit-promotion semantics are preserved.
A few smaller wins on the non-tick path:
- Drop `int(np.fabs(stride) * stride_sign)` (numpy scalar dance) in favor of explicit sign-aware Python int multiplication, mirroring the tick path.
- Convert `_warn_about_deprecated_aliases` and `_validate_to_offset_alias` to `cdef`. Both are only called from `to_offset`, so no public-API impact.
- Cache `alias.upper()` in `_validate_to_offset_alias` (was being called up to 3 times).
- Precompute `c_PERIOD_AND_OFFSET_DEPR_FREQSTR.values()` as a frozenset (was an O(n) values-view lookup per call).
- Walk the regex split by index rather than `zip(split[0::4], split[1::4], split[2::4])` — drops three list-slice copies and the zip object on every call.
Also adds asv benchmarks (`ToOffset`, `ToOffsetPassthrough`) since none existed for `to_offset`.

- The `# FIXME: this should be totally avoidable` comment in `_str_map` has been there since 2019 (added in #29314).
- The regex it sits next to matches CPython's standard ""takes/missing ... positional arguments"" `TypeError` message — that format hasn't changed in any Python release in the intervening 7 years.

- Replace the FIXME with a NOTE explaining what the regex is actually keying on (CPython's arity-error message for a user-supplied callable like `repl`).

`pd.concat` compares the same logical pair of indexes twice:
1. `union_indexes` → `all_indexes_same` (`indexes/api.py:337`) walks the inputs pairwise (`first.equals(idx)` for each other), and returns `indexes[0]` when they all match.
2. Then `_get_result` (`reshape/concat.py:652`) re-runs `new_labels.equals(obj_labels)` per obj to decide whether reindexing is needed — re-asking the same question.
In the common case where input frames are separately-constructed with content-equal-but-identity-distinct column indexes, ~50% of all `Index.equals` calls under `pd.concat` are this duplicate.
### Numbers (per call, inputs pre-built outside the timer)
After"" estimated by short-circuiting duplicate `(id(self), id(other))` pairs with a per-concat memoization cache.
Patterns that build frames separately then concat them: `pd.concat([pd.read_csv(f) for f in files])` and equivalents.

`groupby.apply` and `pd.concat([df.iloc[a:b] for ...])` see **zero** `Index.equals` calls (slices share the parent's Index identity, `_get_distinct_objs` collapses them) — this change is a no-op for those.

Return an `all_equal` flag from `union_indexes` when it takes the `all_indexes_same`-True branch, and thread it through `_get_combined_index` → `new_axes` → `_get_result`. When True, `_get_result` skips the per-obj `equals` and uses empty indexers.

Happy to put up a PR.",,"{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65393/reactions', 'total_count': 2, '+1': 2, '-1': 0, 'laugh': 0, 'hooray': 0, 'confused': 0, 'heart': 0, 'rocket': 0, 'eyes': 0}",https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65393/timeline,,,"{'total': 0, 'completed': 0, 'percent_completed': 0}","{'blocked_by': 0, 'total_blocked_by': 0, 'blocking': 0, 'total_blocking': 0}",,

- `TemporalValidator.is_valid_skipna` only recognized `None` and `NaN` as generic nulls, so `infer_dtype` on a list of `Timestamp` / `Timedelta` values mixed with `pd.NA` returned `""date""` (since `Timestamp` is a `date` subclass) or `""mixed""` instead of `""datetime""` / `""timedelta""`. Add `value is C_NA` to the generic-null check.
- Extend the parametrized `test_infer_dtype_datetime_with_na` and `test_infer_dtype_timedelta_with_na` to cover `pd.NA` alongside `pd.NaT` and `np.nan`.

- The `pyarrow` `test_infer_dtype_pyarrow_dtype` xfail had a misleading reason that pointed at this `pd.NA` handling. The test still fails for an unrelated reason (`infer_dtype(arrow_array)` returns `""datetime64""` via `_TYPE_MAP` while `infer_dtype(list)` returns `""datetime""` from the value-based path), so the xfail is kept but with a more accurate reason.

- `test_assert_index_equal_different_inferred_types` previously relied on `Index([NA, np.datetime64(\""nat\"", \""ns\"")])` falling through to object dtype with `inferred_type == \""mixed\""`. With `pd.NA` now recognized as a generic null, that constructs a `DatetimeIndex`. Force `dtype=object` on both indexes so the test continues to exercise the `inferred_type are different` assertion path.

Removes the `_offset_map` dict cache inside `_get_offset` (offsets.pyx). It was added in 2012 (commit `0cc1a3d`) when offset construction was slow enough that caching alias→offset lookups was worth it.
Today the cache buys very little:
- Construction itself is ~0.3 µs; the cache saves ~0.3 µs at the bare-`_get_offset` level and ~0.5 µs at the realistic `to_offset(non-tick)` level (~5–25%).
- Tick aliases (`D`, `h`, `min`, `s`, `ms`, `us`, `ns`) bypass `_get_offset` entirely, so the cache never serves them.

- `to_offset` itself never returned cached identity even with the cache, because the trailing `offset * int(np.fabs(stride) * stride_sign)` step always produces a fresh instance. So `to_offset(name) is to_offset(name)` was already `False` for every name, and equality is preserved.
After the change, `_get_offset` is just `prefix_mapping[split[0]]._from_name(*split[1:])` with the existing error wrapping.

`Timedelta.total_seconds()` combines days, seconds, and microseconds but silently drops the sub-microsecond nanosecond component. For example, ``Timedelta(nanoseconds=999).total_seconds()`` returned ``0.0`` instead of ``9.99e-7``, and ``Timedelta(""1us 500ns"").total_seconds()`` returned ``1e-6`` instead of ``1.5e-6``. The fix adds the missing ``self._ns / 1_000_000_000`` term.

A pre-existing ``# TODO: add nanos/1e9?`` comment had flagged this. The existing ``test_total_seconds_scalar`` regression test silently passed because ``assert_almost_equal`` defaults to 5-decimal tolerance, hiding the up-to-456 ns error.
> Stacked on top of GH-65388 (PERF: Speed up Timedelta.total_seconds). Until that merges, this PR will show both commits in the diff.
- [x] New regression test ``test_total_seconds_includes_nanoseconds`` covering positive and negative nanosecond residuals
- [x] Full ``pandas/tests/scalar/timedelta/`` and ``pandas/tests/arrays/test_timedeltas.py`` suites pass locally (723 tests)

- `_Timedelta.total_seconds()` reads `_d/_h/_m/_s/_ms/_us` directly instead of going through the `days`, `seconds`, and `microseconds` `@property` descriptors. ~3.3x faster on a microbenchmark (~118 ns → ~36 ns/call).

- Drops the stale `TODO(cython3): make cdef property` comments on `days`/`seconds`/`microseconds`. Cython 3's `cdef property` syntax (`@property cdef inline ... noexcept`) only applies to `extern` cdef classes (e.g. the entries in `cpython/datetime.pxd`); it errors out on regular `cdef class _Timedelta(timedelta)` with ""Cdef functions cannot take arbitrary decorators."" So the TODO is not actionable, and this PR achieves the underlying intent (skip the Python descriptor on internal cdef calls) by reading the typed fields directly.

- Drops the local `cdef extern from ""numpy/ndarraytypes.h""` block in `np_datetime.pxd` that re-declared `npy_datetimestruct`, `NPY_DATETIMEUNIT`, and the 15 `NPY_FR_*` enum members. These have long been exposed by numpy's own Cython `pxd`, so re-declaring them was redundant and a latent maintenance hazard if numpy ever changed the struct layout.
- Resolves the `TODO(cython3)` tag in that file — the project's Cython floor (`>3.1.0` in `pyproject.toml`) has long since cleared the bar.
- `NPY_DATETIME_NAT` is *not* exposed by numpy's `pxd`, so a single-line extern declaration for it remains.
- Consumers (22 `.pyx`/`.pxd` files) do not need to change — they `cimport` these names from `pandas._libs.tslibs.np_datetime`, which transitively re-exports them.

I checked the rest of the codebase — `pandas/_libs/tslibs/util.pxd` is the only other file with `cdef extern from ""numpy/...""` blocks, but the symbols it imports (`PyFloatingArrType_Type`, `PyComplexFloatingArrType_Type`, `PyBoolArrType_Type`, `PyArray_IsIntegerScalar`, `NPY_MIN_INT64`) are not exposed by numpy's `pxd`, so nothing else can be migrated.
- [x] Manual smoke test: `Timestamp`, `Timedelta`, `date_range`, `timedelta_range`, `period_range`, `to_datetime`, `to_timedelta`, `astype('datetime64[ns]')`, `Timestamp.round` all work as expected

Narrow audit of grossly outdated user-guide content. Scope is limited to factually wrong text and stable features still labeled ""experimental"".
- `doc/source/user_guide/10min.rst` — drop ``df2.append`` from the simulated IPython tab-completion block. ``DataFrame.append`` was removed in 2.0.

- `Timestamp.round/floor/ceil` end with `result.tz_localize(self.tz, ambiguous=ambiguous, nonexistent=nonexistent)` when tz-aware, so they really can return `NaT` when `ambiguous='NaT'` or `nonexistent='NaT'`. The previous `-> Self` annotation was wrong, as flagged by an existing `# TODO: round/floor/ceil could return NaT?` comment in `timestamps.pyi`. Each method is now split into two `@overload`s — callers using safe kwargs still get `-> Self`, and only NaT-producing kwargs widen to `Self
column2: NaTType`. Mypy first-match overload resolution means default-kwarg call sites (e.g. `last.ceil(""D"")` in `resample.py`) are unaffected.

column1: - `Timedelta.round/floor/ceil` carried the same TODO, but the implementation has no tz path: `_round` only ever returns `self`, returns `Timedelta._from_value_and_reso(...)`, or raises `OutOfBoundsTimedelta`/`ValueError`. The TODO was stale and is removed without a signature change.

`BaseBlockManager.get_dtypes()` (`pandas/core/internals/managers.py:339`) rebuilds an object array on every call:
dtypes = np.array([blk.dtype for blk in self.blocks], dtype=object)

It's called from a number of hot paths — `df.dtypes`, `DataFrame.fillna` (`generic.py:6391`), `select_dtypes`/`_get_numeric_data`, `DataFrame.apply` (`frame.py:15751`), `unique_dtypes` in repr/info (`generic.py:7136`), and twice per `iloc` setitem (`indexing.py:944, 975`). For wide, fragmented managers each call is O(nblocks) Python iteration plus an object-array allocation.
Caching saves the full cost on cache hits; `df.dtypes` retains ~8 us of unavoidable Series-construction overhead.

Add a `_dtypes_cache` attribute on the manager, populated lazily in `get_dtypes()` and invalidated alongside the existing `_blknos` / `_interleaved_dtype` cache hooks (`managers.py:1411, 1497` and other block-mutation sites). The same invalidation pattern is already in place — adding one more entry is mechanical.

- The cached array must not be mutated in place by callers. A quick audit of callers shows they only read it (equality checks, `.take`, iteration) — no in-place writes — but worth confirming during review.
- `SingleBlockManager` already has only one block; the cache is still cheap and consistent.

Problem: nansem with mask + skipna=False + axis={0,1} returned a scalar
np.nan instead of an array due to an early return that ignored the axis
Fix: Narrowed the early return guard to axis=None only. For axis=0/1,
nanvar already propagates nan correctly per-slice, so the early return

`Series.str.replace("""", ...)` with a PyArrow-backed Series hangs indefinitely, catching kill signals, making the process unresponsive. The hang also reproduces in CI (e.g. skrub-data/skrub#1772).

Root cause: PyArrow's `replace_substring` / `replace_substring_regex` enters an infinite loop when given an empty pattern (apache/arrow#39149). After substituting at position 0, the loop advances by `len("""") = 0` and never moves forward.
A previous fix (GH#56404, GH#56406) guarded against negative `max_replacements`, but empty patterns were not addressed.
In `_arrow_string_mixins._str_replace`, detect `pat == """"` before calling into PyArrow and fall back to element-wise Python processing, which replicates Python's own semantics:
Both `regex=True` (via `re.sub`) and `regex=False` (via `str.replace`) paths are handled. `None` / `NA` values are preserved.

I used Claude Code (claude-sonnet-4-6) to locate the hang site and draft the fix. I reviewed the logic — checking that `re.sub("""", ...)` and `str.replace("""", ...)` produce identical output for the empty-pattern case, and that the `n` / `count` semantics map correctly — before committing.","{'login': 'jbrockmendel', 'id': 8078968, 'node_id': 'MDQ6VXNlcjgwNzg5Njg=', 'avatar_url': 'https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8078968?v=4', 'gravatar_id': '', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel', 'html_url': 'https://github.com/jbrockmendel', 'followers_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/followers', 'following_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/following{/other_user}', 'gists_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/gists{/gist_id}', 'starred_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/starred{/owner}{/repo}', 'subscriptions_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/subscriptions', 'organizations_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/orgs', 'repos_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/repos', 'events_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/events{/privacy}', 'received_events_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/jbrockmendel/received_events', 'type': 'User', 'user_view_type': 'public', 'site_admin': False}","{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65370/reactions', 'total_count': 0, '+1': 0, '-1': 0, 'laugh': 0, 'hooray': 0, 'confused': 0, 'heart': 0, 'rocket': 0, 'eyes': 0}",https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65370/timeline,,,,,,

'merged_at': None}","- closes #40234
`select_dtypes` routed every spec through `infer_dtype_from_object`, collapsing EA and numpy dtypes into a shared `dtype.type` / `issubclass` matching space. This caused several unrelated issues:
- `select_dtypes(""Int64"")` and `select_dtypes(pd.Int64Dtype)` silently matched numpy `int64` columns as well as the Int64 EA (GH-40234).
- `select_dtypes(include=[""datetime64[ns]""])` silently matched Arrow `timestamp[ns][pyarrow]` columns, and `include=[""timestamp[ns][pyarrow]""]` returned empty (GH-59888).
- Passing a parameterized EA class like `pd.ArrowDtype` or `pd.DatetimeTZDtype` as a spec emitted a spurious `UserWarning: Instantiating X without any arguments`.
Resolves the conflation by routing user-facing dtype specs into three distinct buckets:
- **Numpy scalar types** (`np.int64`, `np.dtype(""int64"")`) — match by `issubclass` on `dtype.type`. Storage-specific.
- **`ExtensionDtype` requests** — class → match any instance of that class (kind), instance → exact `==` (identity).
- **Kind-name strings** (`""int64""`, `""float64""`, `""datetime""`, `""bool""`, ...) — match by `(dtype.kind, dtype.itemsize)` across numpy, masked, and Arrow backends.

The third bucket gives us the kind reading reviewers asked for: `select_dtypes(""int64"")` selects every int64 column regardless of storage (numpy, Int64Dtype, ArrowDtype), `select_dtypes(""datetime"")` selects naive numpy, tz-aware DatetimeTZDtype, and Arrow timestamp columns alike. Type-based specs (`np.int64`, `pd.Int64Dtype()`) remain storage-/identity-specific.

EA-parseable strings unify under one rule: bracket-free strings (`""category""`, `""string""`, `""Int64""`) are class (kind) requests, bracketed strings (`""datetime64[ns, UTC]""`, `""timestamp[ns][pyarrow]""`) are instance (identity) requests. The hand-maintained legacy alias map shrinks to just `""datetimetz""`/`""datetime64tz""` (the strings that don't parse via `pandas_dtype`).
As a natural consequence, passing an EA class (e.g. `pd.ArrowDtype`, `pd.DatetimeTZDtype`) now works as a ""kind"" request matching any instance of that class, with no warning.

`get_dummies` previously relied on the `ArrowDtype` → `numpy_dtype` unwrap to include Arrow string columns via `include=[""object""]`. Its two `select_dtypes` calls are replaced by a local `_is_encodable` predicate fed to `BlockManager._get_data_subset`, preserving existing behavior (GH-56273) without reintroducing the cross-class match.
- EA instance specs (e.g. `DatetimeTZDtype(""UTC"")`, `ArrowDtype(pa.int64())`) now match by exact dtype equality. To match ""any tz"" / ""any Arrow int"", pass the class instead.
- `select_dtypes(np.int64)` (the numpy *type*) no longer matches `Int64` EA columns — but `select_dtypes(""int64"")` (the string *kind*) selects both, plus Arrow int64.
- `select_dtypes(""datetime"")` / `""datetime64""` now match `DatetimeTZDtype` and Arrow timestamp columns in addition to numpy `datetime64`. Previously matched numpy only.

- [x] New tests for GH-40234, GH-59888 (both halves + symmetric Arrow-int case), parameterized-class warning elimination, class-as-kind semantics for `ArrowDtype` / `DatetimeTZDtype` / `CategoricalDtype`, kind-string matching across backends for int64/float64/datetime, and the Python-`str` route under `future.infer_string`
- [x] Existing `test_select_dtypes.py` suite passes (53 pass, 4 existing skips)
- [x] Broader pass across `frame/`, `dtypes/`, `extension/`, `arrays/`, `reshape/`, `plotting/`, `window/`, `io/json/` (85,341 pass, no regressions)

The SAS7BDAT cython parser's inner loop copies bytes from the page buffer into ``byte_chunk`` one byte at a time via ``buf_get`` (a memoryview-indexed read with an assert). The vast majority of SAS numeric columns are stored as full-precision 8-byte doubles, where this loop can be replaced by a single 8-byte load/store. Shorter widths (3–7 bytes) keep the existing per-byte path.
156 real-world SAS7BDAT files, best of 5–25 iterations per file, on macOS / Apple Silicon:

column6: max

column1: file                          size_kb     rows  cols  str   before    after  speedup

The win scales with ``nrows × n_full_precision_float_cols``. Files with mostly short-width float columns see no change. Small (<100 KB) files show occasional 5–20 µs regressions on sub-millisecond reads — likely the cost of the extra ``if lngt == 8`` branch when the fast path never triggers. 'merged_at': '2026-04-28T21:12:34Z'}","## Summary

Follow-up to GH#48323 / #64753. The deprecation warning added in `_AtIndexer._warn_if_expanding` only checked the row axis, so `df.at[existing_row, new_col] = val` silently expanded the columns without firing the `Pandas4Warning`.

- `_warn_if_expanding` now checks both `key[0]` against `obj.index` and `key[1]` against `obj.columns` for ndim=2. First axis that would expand fires the warning; early return avoids double-warning when both are new.
- Added three tests in `TestAtSetItemWithExpansion` covering: new column on a plain-columns frame, new column on a frame with MultiIndex rows, and new tuple column on a frame with MultiIndex columns.
